coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for determination of nickel ions and morin from aqueous solution simultaneously
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Changxin
Jiao, Yuan
Gao, Yifang
Liu, Xiaona
Qian, Tianwei
coredata.dc:description: Considering the significant impact of heavy metals on environment and food safety inspection in trace determination, the orange photoluminescent carbon dots (O-CDs) was innovative designed by promoting the co-hydrothermal reaction of polyethyleneimine and resazurin. The produced O-CDs exhibit exceptional optical performance, advantageous water solubility and outstanding photostability. It is worth emphasizing that the proposed O-CDs has significant advantages in constructing sequential sensing platform for sensitive determination of Ni2+ and morin. Simultaneous taking Ni2+ for analytes and fluorescence quenching agents on the O-CDs sensing platforms, a good linear relationship can be received in the range of 5–50 μM with satisfactory sensitivity. Subsequently, morin can effectively promote the fluorescence recovery of O-CDs@Ni2+ on account of stronger binding force between morin and Ni2+. Based on the above results, effective recovery of 97.26 %-100.90 % and 98.24 %-101.99 % is observed towards nickel ion and morin in real food samples. These findings provide an original strategy for the construction of long-wavelength emission carbon dots, which provides a pathway for the determination of typical pollutants based on efficient and robust nanosensors in the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-wavelength emission
Multifunctional carbon dots
Sequence detection
Nickel ions
Morin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of potential interferers for Ni2+ and morin
Detection of Ni2+ and morin based on O-CDs
Analysis of Ni2+ and morin in real samples
Optical performance of O-CDs to Ni2+
Recovery of Ni2+ in food samples
Optical performance of O-CDs@Ni2+ to morin
Recovery of morin in milk sample
Response mechanism of O-CDs to Ni2+ and morin

This work was financially supported by the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302124056, No. 202103021223088), and Science and technology innovation project of colleges and universities in Shanxi Province (2021L029, 2021L030), Transverse research project of Taiyuan University of Technology ( RH2200002532 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of O-CDs, inset right: HR-TEM of O-CDs, inset left: Size distribution diagram of O-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of O-CDs. (c) Full XPS survey spectra of the O-CDs, high-resolution (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s XPS spectra of the O-CDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra, PL excitation, and emission spectra of O-CDs. (b) Excitation-emission mapping of O-CDs.
(a) Fluorescence response of O-CDs to Ni2+ (0–180 μM). (b) Relationship between the ΔF of O-CDs with the concentration of Ni2+ at 0–180 μM. Inset: linearity of response.
(a) Fluorescence response of O-CDs@Ni2+ to morin (0–120 μM). (b) Relationship between the ΔF of O-CDs@Ni2+ with the concentration of morin at 0–120 μM. Inset: linearity of response.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ni2+ and O-CDs@Ni2+ with morin respectively. (b) The fluorescence emission decay curve of the O-CDs, O-CDs@Ni2+ and O-CDs@Ni2+ with morin and relevant data.
Schematic illustration of innovative preparation of O-CDs and its application in food safety inspection.
Detection Ni2+ in lettuce and chocolate samples based on O-CDs.
Detection morin in milk sample based on O-CDs@Ni2+.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for determination of nickel ions and morin from aqueous solution simultaneously

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecological Security in Fenhe River Basin, Shanxi Engineering Research Center of Low Carbon Remediation for Water and Soil Pollution in Yellow River Basin, Jinzhong 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering
Shanxi Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecological Security in Fenhe River Basin
Shanxi Engineering Research Center of Low Carbon Remediation for Water and Soil Pollution in Yellow River Basin

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecological Security in Fenhe River Basin, Shanxi Engineering Research Center of Low Carbon Remediation for Water and Soil Pollution in Yellow River Basin, Jinzhong 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the significant impact of heavy metals on environment and food safety inspection in trace determination, the orange photoluminescent carbon dots (O-CDs) was innovative designed by promoting the co-hydrothermal reaction of polyethyleneimine and resazurin. The produced O-CDs exhibit exceptional optical performance, advantageous water solubility and outstanding photostability. It is worth emphasizing that the proposed O-CDs has significant advantages in constructing sequential sensing platform for sensitive determination of Ni2+ and morin. Simultaneous taking Ni2+ for analytes and fluorescence quenching agents on the O-CDs sensing platforms, a good linear relationship can be received in the range of 5–50 μM with satisfactory sensitivity. Subsequently, morin can effectively promote the fluorescence recovery of O-CDs@Ni2+ on account of stronger binding force between morin and Ni2+. Based on the above results, effective recovery of 97.26 %-100.90 % and 98.24 %-101.99 % is observed towards nickel ion and morin in real food samples. These findings provide an original strategy for the construction of long-wavelength emission carbon dots, which provides a pathway for the determination of typical pollutants based on efficient and robust nanosensors in the field of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are emphasized among common pollutants on the basis of their high-toxicity [1], non-degradability [2] and bioaccumulation effects [3], and the critical determination of heavy metals have become one of the essential research fields [4]. Sufficient evidence has certificated that excessive nickel ions (Ni2+) can irreversibly damage the human immune system andeventually result in malignant health problem [5] such as nickel-eczema, acute pneumonia [6], asthma [7], and even respiratory cancer [8]. At present, the commonly used methods for the detection of Ni2+ include flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10], graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS) [11] and flame photometry [12] etc. These instruments provide reliable methods for the detection of Ni2+, but there are also come with some drawbacks such as complex operation, expensive equipment, and inability to achieve in-situ detection [13–15]. Morin, a prominent flavonoid compound, has attracted considerable attention for its superior anti-diabetic, antioxidant, antibacterial, and even anti-cancer properties [16–18]. Nonetheless, excessive consumption of morin can lead to gastrointestinal discomfort, liver damage, and even poses potential risks such as anticoagulant effects [19]. At present, different methods for the determination of morin have been established, including electrochemical method, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fluorescence method and so on [20]. More recently, fluorometry has been recognized as a compliment approach due to its advantages in simple operation, favorable selectivity, low cost and analysis convenience [21–24]. Taking into account the above considerations, innovatively designing a sensitive fluorescence sensor based on the fluorescence method for the accurate detection of Ni2+ and morin is of great significance in food safety inspection [25].

As a new type of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial [26], carbon dots (CDs) have been intensively utilized in selective recognition of heavy metal ions based on their marvelous photoluminescence [27–29], excellent photostability, low toxicity and exceptional biocompatibility [30–32]. For instance, Yuan et al. [33] prepared yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) choosing VB2 and citrate as precursors, and further demonstrated its specific responsiveness to Hg2+ with excellent linear ranges and LOD. Ma et al. [34] obtained blue emission mopan persimmons carbon quantum dots (MP-CQDs) from fresh Mopan persimmons by the hydrothermal method, which exhibits fluorescence quenching performance in Fe3+ determination. Different from these metal ions such as Fe3+, Hg2+ or Cu2+ etc., the distinctive fluorescence sensor based on CDs devoted to Ni2+ has not been extensively explored. Li et al. [35] reported the solvent-free pyrolysis method for blue fluorescence CDs starting from tannic acid and demonstrated its application in trace detection of Ni2+. However, the fluorescence associated with these CDs-based nanosensors for Ni2+ are mostly limited to short wavelength regions and present single functional response mode. Dang et al. [36] designed functionalized N-CDs as the label-free nanoprobe for the sequential detection of Cu2+ and ciprofloxacin, demonstrating the enormous potential of CDs as multifunctional probes for detecting metal ions and organic in “on–off-on” mode. Therefore, the development of long-wavelength emitting carbon dots [37] and further construction of CDs-based multifunctional fluorescent sensor concern of Ni2+ remains a gargantuan challenge.

In this regard, an original fluorescent “on–off−on” nanosensor based on O-CDs was innovatively developed with polyethyleneimine and resazurin as precursors. The obtained O-CDs showcase exceptional fluorescent capabilities with satisfactory fluorescence quantum yield. Upon introducing a certain amount of Ni2+, the fluorescence of O-CDs exhibits a predictable decrease accompanied by a lower detection limit. Intriguingly, the addition of morin counteracts this quenching effect thanks to a stronger interaction with Ni2+, thereby revitalizing the fluorescence of O-CDs@Ni2+. Moreover, the efficacy of innovatively designed platform has been demonstrated in the simultaneous detection of Ni2+ and morin within food samples, underscoring its potential as a versatile nanomaterial for multifaceted detection applications. Such advancements offer a fresh perspective on food safety inspections, positioning the synthesized O-CDs as a valuable tool for environmental monitoring and analysis (Scheme 1 ).

Polyethyleneimine, resazurin, assorted metal salts, antibiotics and rhodamine B were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Morin was purchased from RhawnCo., Ltd. The phosphate buffer solution (PBS) under different PH was obtained by confusing 0.02 M Na2HPO4 with 0.02 M NaH2PO4, followed by adjustment with 0.1 M HCl and NaOH. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ.cm) utilized in the general experiment was provided by the rephile genie water system.

In the present work, O-CDs was originally obtained through hydrothermal reaction of resazurin and polyethyleneimine. Primly, 0.5 g of polyethyleneimine and 0.05 g of resazurin were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, then the solution was sealed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave tube and react at 200 ℃ for 8 h. The obtained suspensions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then 15 mL of the O-CDs solution was further dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a MWCO of 0–500 Da. Sequentially, the dispersion was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane and ultimately freeze-dried to obtain solid-state O-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of potential interferers for Ni2+ and morin
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of O-CDs were investigated by replace Ni2+ with 0.5 mmol·L−1 different metal ions (K+, Na+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+) and anions (F−, Br−, Cl−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, ClO−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, S2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, HSO3 −, P2O7 4−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −), the other operating procedures are consistent with Ni2+.

The anti-interference performance of O-CDs@Ni2+ to morin was conducted by choosing different types of antibiotics with the concentration of 0.05 mmol·L−1 (Amoxicillin, Metronidazole, Sertraline, Trimethoprim, Azithromycin, Tea Polyphenols, Sulfamethoxazole, Tinidazole, Penicillin, Ornidazole, Kanamycin, Tetracycline, Rutin, Clindamycin, Cefradine, Clarithromycin) and repeating the same fluorescence operation as morin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ni2+ and morin based on O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolved 20 µL O-CDs solution (0.2 mg·mL−1) in 2.0 mL of pH = 8.0 PBS solution, then different concentrations of Ni2+ in the range of 0–180 µM were added into the O-CDs dispersion . For all measurements, the fluorescence emission intensity was measured at 592 nm with an excitation wavelength of 575 nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set to 3 nm and all operations were repeated three times.
Sequentially, different concentrations of morin ranging from 0 to 120 μM were added to the O-CDs@Ni2+ system and other fluorescence operations are the same as above.

All food samples related in the experiment including lettuce, chocolate, and milk were purchased from local supermarket. Briefly, lettuce and chocolate were adopted for the determination of Ni2+ in real samples. The lettuce and chocolate were chosen for detect Ni2+. Mashing 10 g of lettuce in a mortar and then dissolving it with 10 mL of ultrapure water, the suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min following filtered by 0.22 µm microporous membrane. Furthermore, 4 mL of lettuce extracting solution was blended with 16 mL of 0.02 M PBS solution and adjust its pH to 8.0. Correspondingly, the chocolate sample also undergone the same procedure as lettuce, 1g of chocolate was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, then extracted the supernatant after centrifugation and filtered by 0.22 µm microporous membrane. Moreover, 4 mL of chocolate extracting solution was blended with 16 mL of 0.02 M PBS solution and adjust its pH to 8.0.

Choosing milk samples for the recovery determination of morin. The milk sample was preliminarily prepared by filtering to remove suspended impurities, and further centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove the proteins. Afterward, 5 mL of supernatant was blended with 15 mL PBS solution (pH = 8.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ni2+ and morin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed O-CDs for food safety inspections was evaluated by assessing the recovery of Ni2+ in lettuce and chocolate. The fluorescence measurement was conducted by introducing 20 µL of 0.2 mg·mL−1 O-CDs solution into 2 mL lettuce and chocolate sample, then collected the fluorescence spectra with different concentrations of Ni2+ (10, 15, 20, and 25 µM) in sequence. Furthermore, the practicality of the developed O-CDs@Ni2+ was further assessed through the standard addition method using morin in a milk sample. Milk samples treated with various concentrations of morin were analyzed to determine their fluorescence spectra, and other fluorescence operations are the same as above.

The relative recovery (RR%) was calculated based on the following equation:
RR % = [
(
C found
-
C real
)
/
C added
] × 1 00 %

where Cfound is the concentration measured in actual sample with addition of the quantitative standard, Creal is the concentration of the analyte in real sample, and Cadded is the concentration of the quantitative standard added to the actual sample.

The dimension and morphology were illustrated in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) indicates the homogenous and well-dispersed characteristic of prepared O-CDs with an average diameter of 3.15 nm. The HRTEM image of O-CDs demonstrates the clear lattice spacing at 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1 a).

The FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to identify the functional groups present on the surface of O-CDs (Fig. 1b). The absorption peak at 3436 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of –OH, and the absorption bands at 2985 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1 are associated with C-H stretching vibration. Moreover, the sharp peak at 1598 cm−1 and 1368 cm−1 are in connection with the typical stretching vibration of -C=O and -C-N= respectively [38]. Furthermore, we conducted FT-IR spectroscopy comparisons of O-CDs, resazurin, and polyethyleneimine (Fig. S1). It is found that the characteristic peak of polyethyleneimine at 1492 cm−1 (δN-H) and the absorption peak of resazurin at 1368 cm−1 (ν-C-N=) were both present in the O-CDs structure, indirectly proving the successful generation of O-CDs. Additionally, XPS spectra was conducted to determine the elemental composition of O-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the prepared O-CDs is mainly composed of C, N and O elements with atomic ratio of 65.56, 19.14, and 15.30 %. The high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 1d) presents three functional groups in C=C/C-C (283.3 eV), C-N/C-O (284.3 eV) and C=O (286.5 eV). The N1s of the O-CDs in Fig. 1e exhibits three peaks in 397.6 eV, 398.1 eV and 399.0 eV, belonging to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. Furthermore, the peak of O1s can be decomposed into two peaks at C=O (529.9 eV) and C-O (531.0 eV) [39]. Based on the results presented by FT-IR and XPS, it can be determined that the surface of O-CDs is abundant in carbonyl and amino groups.

The optical characteristics of O-CDs were further verified by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As illustrated by the black line in Fig. 2 a, there exhibit two distinct absorption peaks in 199 nm and 400 nm, which originate from the π-π* transition of aromatic C=C and the n-π* transition of C=O or C-N, respectively. The comparison of UV–Vis absorption spectra indicates significant differences between the absorption peaks of O-CDs and resazurin (Fig. S2). In the UV–Vis absorption of O-CDs, the absorption peaks of resazurin at 282 nm and 380 nm are conspicuously absent, and the adsorption peak at 600 nm shifts to 566 nm. The relatively higher proportion of π-π* transitions dominated by the sp2 structure in O-CDs skeleton indicates that resazurin has successfully participated in the aggregation, resulting in the formation of O-CDs with a larger conjugated structure. Moreover, the optimal excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelength are determined to be 575 nm and 592 nm as displayed by the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2b). It is noteworthy that O-CDs present excitation-independent property as the excitation wavelength range from 300 nm to 600 nm, which may be ascribed to the homogeneous surface state of O-CDs. Additionally, the relative quantum yield of O-CDs was determined as 14.0 % (Fig. S3).

The photoluminescence performance of O-CDs towards different conditions was further evaluated. It is commendable that the FL intensity of O-CDs at 592 nm demonstrate only slightly variation upon continuous exposure to 575 nm xenon arc light for 60 min (Fig. S4). Moreover, the FL intensity of O-CDs remains stable upon exposed to NaCl at different concentrations (0–2 mol·L−1), revealing the exceptional salt tolerance of produced O-CDs (Fig. S5). When the pH increases from 2 to 13, O-CDs exhibit pH-dependent behavior and present optimal fluorescence intensity at pH = 8.0 (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance of O-CDs to Ni2+
ce:section.ce:para: The responsiveness of O-CDs to various metal ions (K+, Na+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ni2+) and anions (F−, Br−, Cl−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, ClO−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, S2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, HSO3 −, P2O7 4−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −) were conducted and illustrated in Fig. S7. The histogram certificates that the O-CDs achieved the best response to Ni2+ and the FL intensity at 592 nm is significantly reduced in contrast to other ions. As presented in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence of O-CDs decreases continuously with the introduction of Ni2+ ranging from 0 to 180 µM. Correspondingly, the relationship between the FL intensity at 592 nm and the concentration of Ni2+ is further presented by the ExpDec1 function, thereby a good linear relationship can be acquired within the range of 5–50 µM, the corresponding linear equation are ΔF = 49.7427C + 765.4321, R2 = 0.9955 (ΔF = F0-F, F0 and F represent the FL intensity at 592 nm in the absence and presence of Ni2+.) The limit of detection (LOD) is determined as 42.2 nM (Fig. 3b). Table S1 lists the comparison of methods for detecting Ni2+. Traditional methods such as ICP-OES, FAAS, and ICP-MS offer higher detection accuracy but typically require more complex sample processing and have higher operational costs. In contrast, fluorescence technology has significant advantages in terms of response time, operational cost, and selectivity. Additionally, a comparative study of different sensors based on fluorescence is presented in Table S2. Overall, it can be comprehensively determined that the proposed O-CDs exhibit longer emission wavelengths and demonstrate excellent performance in terms of detection linear range and limit of LOD compared to other fluorescent sensors, demonstrating its potential as a versatile nanomaterial for multifaceted detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of Ni2+ in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the proposed fluorescent O-CDs in food safety inspection was investigated by measuring the recovery of Ni2+ in lettuce and chocolate. Various concentrations of Ni2+ (10, 15, 20, and 25 µM) were spiked into the processed food samples, the recovery of Ni2+ in lettuce and chocolate are determined as 97.26 %-100.90 % and 99.57 %-100.80 % with RSD ≤ 5.90 % (Table 1 ). Moreover, ICP-OES and UV–Vis were further employed to validate the efficiency and accuracy of O-CDs in the fluorescence recognition of Ni2+ and morin. As presented in Table S3 and Table S4, the recovery of Ni2+ in lettuce and chocolate are determined as 91.93 %-105.08 % and 91.86 %-102.05 % determined by ICP-OES and UV–Vis, respectively. Comprehensive analysis indicates that the fluorescence method constructed by O-CDs possesses relatively high sensitivity and accuracy. It is worth emphasizing that both lettuce and chocolate have achieved good recovery rates within the established response range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance of O-CDs@Ni2+ to morin
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of O-CDs@Ni2+ composite system was deeply evaluated by introducing 180 µM Ni2+ into O-CDs solution. Incubating O-CDs@Ni2+ solution with 0.05 mmol·L−1 different types of antibiotics (Amoxicillin, Metronidazole, Sertraline, Trimethoprim, Azithromycin, Tea Polyphenols, Sulfamethoxazole, Tinidazole, Penicillin, Ornidazole, Kanamycin, Tetracycline, Rutin, Clindamycin, Cefradine, Clarithromycin, Morin) to certificate the selectivity of O-CDs@Ni2+ preliminarily. As illustrated in Fig. S8, the FL intensity of O-CDs@Ni2+ can be effectively restored in the presence of morin, whereas others do not. Further investigation was conducted into native fluorescence behavior of morin. The results indicate that morin exhibits only a weak emission peak at 416 nm under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Additionally, no significant fluorescence was observed at morin under the optimal excitation wavelength of 575 nm for O-CDs, confirming that morin does not interfere with the recognition behavior of fluorescent sensors constructed by O-CDs (Fig. S9). Moreover, the variation in FL intensity of O-CDs@Ni2+ as the concentration of morin were described in Fig. 4 a. It is obvious that the FL intensity of O-CDs@Ni2+ can recovers positively with the increase of morin concentration. The change in FL intensity at 592 nm as a function of morin concentration indicates an excellent linear relationship in 5–60 μM, and the corresponding linear equation are ΔF = 48.1940C + 249.6390, R2 = 0.9957 (ΔF = F-F0, F0 and F represent the FL intensity at 592 nm in the absence and presence of morin.) with detection limit of 37.3 nM (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, as-synthesized O-CDs@Ni2+ have a wide detection range and the lower LOD in long wave-emission region for the detection of morin compared with other CDs-based fluorescence sensors described in other literatures (Table S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of morin in milk sample
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the constructed nanosensor based on O-CDs@Ni2+ was further evaluated by the standard addition of morin in milk sample. Table 2 illustrates the applicability of O-CDs@Ni2+ in the determination of morin in milk sample and the recovery of different concentrations of morin in milk is calculated as 98.24 %-101.99 %, RSD ≤ 5.09 %. Furthermore, the recovery rate of morin in milk determined by UV–Vis spectroscopy ranged from 97.20 % to 101.20 % (Table S6), verifying the feasibility of the constructed O-CDs@Ni2+ for morin detection. The exceedingly good performance of as-developed O-CDs@Ni2+ to morin distinctly demonstrates the tremendous application potential of prepared nanosensor in food safety inspection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of O-CDs to Ni2+ and morin
ce:section.ce:para: Comparing the UV–Vis absorption spectra of O-CDs before and after adding Ni2+, the shoulder peak of O-CDs at 254 nm is evidently disappeared (Fig. 5 a). However, the absorption peak at 254 nm can be effectively restored accompanied by the generation of a new absorption peak at 326 nm while introducing morin into the mixed solution of O-CDs@Ni2+. Moreover, the zeta potential is supplemented to elucidate the response mechanism of the proposed O-CDs. As shown in Fig. S10, O-CDs exhibit a weak positive potential in + 3.03 mV due to the presence of amino groups on the surface of O-CDs. However, the zeta potential of O-CDs@Ni2+ demonstrates a slight increase from + 3.03 mV to + 3.50 mV due to the formation of ground-state complex between Ni2+ and O-CDs, consuming surface functional groups such as amino groups. More profoundly, the competitive binding of morin to Ni2+ results in a substantial increase in the zeta potential of O-CDs@Ni2+@morin to + 18.73 mV. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ni2+ and O-CDs@Ni2+@morin are measured as 3.37 ns, 3.16 ns and 3.03 ns (Fig. 5b), which demonstrate no significant change. Comprehensive above information, it can be summarized that O-CDs undergoes a static quenching mechanism in the discrimination process of Ni2+ due to the formation of ground-state complex between O-CDs and Ni2+. Upon introducing morin into the mixed solution of O-CDs@Ni2+, Ni2+ can be competitively captured by morin based on its stronger binding ability with morin, thereby significantly restoring the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs.

On the whole, orange photoluminescent carbon dots have been innovatively developed with polyethyleneimine and resazurin as reaction precursors. As-prepared O-CDs demonstrate specific selectivity and sensitivity to Ni2+ over other competitive interference, which can achieve accurate determination of Ni2+ in actual samples of lettuce and chocolate. The fluorescence sensing platform based on O-CDs@Ni2+ was further constructed for the determination of morin with satisfactory sensitivity, and simultaneously achieving the analysis of morin concentration in milk sample. Results acquired in this study distinctly certificate the multifunctional applications of O-CDs and the advantage in specific detection of Ni2+ and morin.

Changxin Gu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yuan Jiao: Resources, Investigation. Yifang Gao: Resources, Investigation. Xiaona Liu: Resources, Investigation. Tianwei Qian: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202303021221041, No. 20210302124056, No. 202103021223088), and Science and technology innovation project of colleges and universities in Shanxi Province (2021L029, 2021L030), Transverse research project of Taiyuan University of Technology (RH2200002532).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110317.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small organic molecules as fluorescent sensors for the detection of highly toxic heavy metal cations in portable water
J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Iftikhar, I. Parveen, Ayesha, A. Mazhar, M.S. Iqbal, G.M. Kamal, F. Hafeez, A.L. Pang, M. Ahmadipour, Small organic molecules as fluorescent sensors for the detection of highly toxic heavy metal cations in portable water, Journal of Environmental Chemical Engineering, 11 (2023) 109030. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2022.109030

J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Guo, Y. Sun, Z. Li, S. Feng, R. Yang, L. Qu, Detection, detoxification, and removal of multiply heavy metal ions using a recyclable probe enabled by click and declick chemistry, Journal of Hazardous Materials, 423 (2022) 127242. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.127242

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification of the antagonistic and synergistic effects of Pb2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ bioaccumulation by living Bacillus subtilis biomass using XGBoost and SHAP
